content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
916 322 3660                322 5601           322 6441              322 6444

October 11, 1977                                                    77-10-069

Mrs. Winston D. Minor
958 Alta Vista Drive
Vista, California  92083

Dear Mrs. Minor:

     Thank you for your letter of September 15, 1977, in which
you raise the issue of whether a school district board trustee
must disqualify himself or herself from participating in deci-
sions of the board relating to determinations concerning salary
or other benefits to school district employees when the spouse
of the trustee is an employee of the district.

     Government Code §97100 provides:

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to in-
fluence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

     Government Code §87103(c) provides:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of §87100 if it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the decision will have a material finan-
cial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on:

     ... (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course
of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value received by or promised to
the public official within twelve months prior to
the time when the decision is made; ...

     ... For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
ment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, . . . .

Mrs. Winston D. Minor
October 11, 1977
Page Two

Thus, under the situation presented it may appear that
the trustee's decision on raising or lowering the salary
level of district employees, including his or her spouse,
would result in prohibited conduct under Government Code
§§87100 and 87103, above, since a source of income to the
trustee is his or her spouse's salary.

However, Government Code §82030(b)(2) excludes from the
definition of income under the Political Reform Act "...
salary ... from a state or local government agency ...;".
This exemption effectively insulates school district trustees
from the requirement to disqualify themselves from decisions
which would affect their spouse's income, or source of income,
under the Political Reform Act.

Thus, district trustees, under normal circumstances,
will be permitted under the Political Reform Act to make or
participate in the making of decisions having a financial
effect upon their income where the source of income is the
school district.

I recognize that this is an anomalous result under the
Political Reform Act. I believe that there was under consideration by the California School Boards Association a proposed amendment to the Act extinguishing this anomaly. Your
questions in this letter regard may be addressed to Joseph
Brooks of the CSBA.

We, of course, make no determination as to whether the
situation presented establishes a common law conflict of
interest beyond the purview of the Political Reform Act.

I trust that this answer has been responsive to your
questions. Please contact me if I can be of further
assistance.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:glb

sections.question: Whether a school district board trustee must disqualify himself or herself from participating in decisions of the board relating to determinations concerning salary or other benefits to school district employees when the spouse of the trustee is an employee of the district.
sections.conclusion: District trustees, under normal circumstances, will be permitted under the Political Reform Act to make or participate in the making of decisions having a financial effect upon their income where the source of income is the school district. This is because Government Code §82030(b)(2) excludes salary from a state or local government agency from the definition of income under the Political Reform Act, effectively insulating school district trustees from the requirement to disqualify themselves from decisions which would affect their spouse's income or source of income.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a school district board trustee disqualify themselves from voting on salary or benefit decisions that would affect their spouse who is employed by the school district?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No, under the Political Reform Act, school district trustees may participate in decisions affecting their spouse's school district salary because government salaries are statutorily exempt from the definition of income triggering disqualification requirements. The FPPC acknowledges this is an anomalous result and notes the California School Boards Association was considering a proposed amendment to address this gap.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard FPPC advice letter format with clear question, legal analysis, and conclusion. Document has no explicit section headers but contains distinct question and conclusion sections. Reference to potential common law conflict of interest noted as outside FPPC scope.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a school district board trustee must disqualify himself or herself from participating in decisions of the board relating to determinations concerning salary or other benefits to school district employees when the spouse of the trustee is an employee of the district.

CONCLUSION: District trustees, under normal circumstances, will be permitted under the Political Reform Act to make or participate in the making of decisions having a financial effect upon their income where the source of income is the school district. This is because Government Code §82030(b)(2) excludes salary from a state or local government agency from the definition of income under the Political Reform Act, effectively insulating school district trustees from the requirement to disqualify themselves from decisions which would affect their spouse's income or source of income.
embedding.first_500_words: October 11, 1977 Mrs. Winston D. Minor 958 Alta Vista Drive Vista, California 92083 Dear Mrs. Minor: Thank you for your letter of September 15, 1977, in which you raise the issue of whether a school district board trustee must disqualify himself or herself from participating in decisions of the board relating to determinations concerning salary or other benefits to school district employees when the spouse of the trustee is an employee of the district. Government Code §87100 provides: No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Government Code §87103(c) provides: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of §87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: ... (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; ... ... For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, .... Thus, under the situation presented it may appear that the trustee's decision on raising or lowering the salary level of district employees, including his or her spouse, would result in prohibited conduct under Government Code §§87100 and 87103, above, since a source of income to the trustee is his or her spouse's salary. However, Government Code §82030(b)(2) excludes from the definition of income under the Political Reform Act "... salary ... from a state or local government agency ...;". This exemption effectively insulates school district trustees from the requirement to disqualify themselves from decisions which would affect their spouse's income, or source of income, under the Political Reform Act. Thus, district trustees, under normal circumstances, will be permitted under the Political Reform Act to make or participate in the making of decisions having a financial effect upon their income where the source of income is the school district. I recognize that this is an anomalous result under the Political Reform Act. I believe that there was under consideration by the California School Boards Association a proposed amendment to the Act extinguishing this anomaly. Your questions in this latter regard may be addressed to Joseph Brooks of the CSBA. We, of course, make no determination as to whether the situation presented establishes a common law conflict of interest beyond the purview of the Political Reform Act. I trust that this answer has been responsive to your questions. Please contact me if I can be of further assistance. Sincerely, Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief Conflicts of Interest Division
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that school district board trustees are not required to disqualify themselves from voting on salary decisions affecting their employed spouses, due to a statutory exemption excluding government agency salaries from the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest definition. The FPPC acknowledges this creates an anomalous result and notes that reform was being considered.
